"source": "## Feature Engineering (53 features)

Every game in training was doubled symmetrically (Team A vs B and Team B vs A) with overtime stats normalized to 40-minute equivalents. This lets the model learn from both perspectives without directional bias.

| Group | # | What | Why it helps |
|-------|---|------|-------------|
| Seeds | 3 | Seed for each team + diff | Strongest single predictor (0.1671 Brier alone) |
| Box scores | 18 | Season-avg points
column4:
fouls (team + opponent) | Baseline team quality signal |
| Late-season form | 2 | Avg point diff in final 2 weeks | Catches teams peaking/slumping into March |
| Elo | 3 | Margin-weighted Elo per team + diff | Dynamic strength with season carry-over |
| GLM quality | 2 | Team quality scores from a statistical model | Estimates strength from point diffs across all games |
| Massey Ordinals | 4 | KenPom rank + avg of ~60 systems | Consensus rankings are very predictive |
| BartTorvik | 18 | AdjOE
column9: Tempo + diffs | **Single biggest improvement (+3.1%)** |\n| Gender | 1 | Men's vs women's flag | 4th most important feature — different upset dynamics |"

column1: "source": "### Why BartTorvik Was the Breakthrough\n\nBefore Level 12
column2: I was computing team strength from raw box scores — season-average points
column5: etc. The problem is that raw box scores don't account for pace or opponent quality. A team that scores 85 points per game against a weak schedule in a fast conference looks the same as one that scores 85 against elite competition. You can try to correct for this with strength-of-schedule adjustments
column6: but you're basically rebuilding what analytics sites like BartTorvik already do better.\n\n[BartTorvik's T-Rank](https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/jswanson/barttorvik-t-rank-2015-2026) provides tempo-adjusted
column7: opponent-adjusted efficiency metrics. The key columns:\n\n- **AdjOE / AdjDE** — Offensive and defensive efficiency (points per 100 possessions
column8: adjusted for opponent strength). This is the gold standard for measuring how good a team actually is on each end of the floor
column9:
stripped of pace and schedule effects.
- **Barthag** — An overall power rating representing a team's expected win probability against an average D1 team. This ended up as the **5th most important feature** in the model.
- **WAB (Wins Above Bubble)** — How many more wins a team has than a bubble-quality team would expect against the same schedule. This captures "are they a legitimate tournament team or did they just beat up on a weak conference?"
- **Tempo** — Adjusted pace of play (possessions per 40 minutes). Fast teams and slow teams create fundamentally different game environments.

The hypothesis was that these pre-computed
column10: expert-quality metrics would give XGBoost strictly better signal than anything I could derive from raw box scores — and that turned out to be right. Adding Torvik features dropped the LOSO Brier from 0.1650 to 0.1599 (3.1% improvement)
column11: the single biggest jump in the entire model evolution. Once Torvik was in the mix
column15: free throw rate) became completely redundant.\n\nThe key decision was feeding these as **training features for XGBoost** rather than building a separate Torvik-based model and ensembling. As direct features
column16: the trees can learn interactions — like \"a team with high AdjDE but a low seed is being underestimated\" or \"Barthag differences matter more in the women's tournament.\" You lose those interactions if you just average two models together."

"source": "### GLM Team Quality\n\nI wanted a simple way to distill all of a team's regular season results into one number. The idea: fit a statistical model where each team gets a \"quality\" coefficient
column2: and the predicted point differential between two teams is just the difference in their coefficients. Teams that consistently beat good opponents by large margins get higher scores. It's a clean way to estimate strength without hand-crafting matchup features."

column2: not probabilities. I needed a way to convert \"this team wins by 8 points\" into \"this team has a 72% chance of winning.\" I tried a few approaches and landed on fitting a degree-5 spline on the out-of-fold margin predictions against binary win indicators. It captures the natural S-curve of the relationship while staying smooth — and unlike a simple logistic function
column3: it handles the tails well (very large margins have diminishing returns on win probability)."

column1: "# At inference: average margins across 22 models
"source": "### Final Blend\n\nWhen I looked at the raw Level 12 predictions
column2: some of them felt extreme — Michigan had an 85% chance of beating Duke
column3: for example. That made me nervous enough to hedge. The submission blended **80% Level 12** (full feature set with BartTorvik) and **20% Level 9** (same architecture but without BartTorvik — just box scores
column6: Massey). The simpler model acts as a regularizer that pulls the most aggressive predictions back toward center.\n\nIn hindsight
column7: Michigan demolished everyone on the way to the title
column8: so maybe those extreme predictions weren't so extreme after all. But the blend still helped overall — it produced the best LOSO Brier score of any configuration I tested."

column1:
"source": "## Feature Importance

XGBoost tracks how much each feature improves predictions across all trees — called "total gain." Higher gain means the feature was more useful for reducing prediction error. The top 10:

| Rank | Feature | Source | What it tells us |
|------|---------|--------|-----------------|
| 1 | Seed_diff | Seeds | Seed gap between teams — by far the strongest signal |
| 2 | elo2_diff | Elo | Elo rating gap — nearly as important as seeds |
| 3 | T2/T1_seed | Seeds | Individual team seeds (not just the diff) |
| 4 | men_women | Gender | Men's vs women's — tournaments behave differently |
| 5 | tv_barthag_diff | BartTorvik | Power rating gap — the top Torvik feature |
| 6 | T1/T2_quality | GLM | Team quality gap |
| 7 | POM_diff | KenPom | KenPom ranking gap |
| 8 | T2_POM | KenPom | Opponent's KenPom rank on its own |
| 9 | MasseyAvg_diff | Massey | Average across ~60 ranking systems |
| 10 | tv_adjoe_diff | BartTorvik | Offensive efficiency gap |

The takeaway: seeds and Elo do the heavy lifting
column2: but the model gets meaningful lift from having multiple independent views of team quality (Torvik
column4: KenPom). Each captures something slightly different
column5: and XGBoost figures out when to trust which signal."

column2: measuring each change honestly with LOSO. Some ideas helped
column3:
some made things worse — both were useful:

| Level | LOSO Brier | What I tried | What I learned |
|-------|-----------|--------------|----------------|
| 1 | 0.1671 | Seed lookup table | Surprisingly hard to beat with anything simple |
| 2 | 0.2273 | Raw win percentage | Made things worse — win% without opponent quality is misleading |
| 3 | 0.1679 | + SOS + margin | Basically just ties seeds |
| 4 | 0.1809 | Elo on its own | Also worse alone — Elo needs to combine with other features |
| 8 | 0.1655 | Replicated a prior 1st-place solution | Gave me a baseline to try to beat |
| 9 | 0.1650 | Better Elo + Massey + late season | Small wins stacking up |
| **12** | **0.1599** | **+ BartTorvik as training features** | **One big jump from bringing in external data** |

The failed experiments (Levels 2 and 4) were actually some of the most instructive. Strong individual signals like win rate or Elo are worse than seeds when used alone — the power comes from combining complementary features that each capture something different about team quality."

column1: "source": "## What Worked\n\n1. **BartTorvik as training features** — 3.1% Brier improvement
column2: the single biggest gain\n2. **Point-diff regression** over classification — richer signal
column3:
better calibration
3. **Margin-weighted Elo** with carry-over — captures dynamic team strength
4. **Massey Ordinals** — consensus of ~60 ranking systems is hard to beat
5. **Spline calibration** — smooth conversion from predicted margins to probabilities
6. **LOSO ensemble** of 22 models — free variance reduction
7. **80/20 blend** — regularizes extreme predictions

## What Didn't Work

- **Conference strength** — redundant with Elo and Massey
- **Box score Four Factors** (eFG%
column6:
FTR) — redundant once BartTorvik is in the mix
- **Probability clipping** — the spline already produced well-calibrated outputs
- **Post-hoc pace compression** (Level 13 experiment) — too blunt as a linear adjustment
- **Clutch factor** (Level 13) — low importance; probably needs play-by-play data to be useful"
